did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated light-weight deep learning models (specifically DenseNet for binary classification and EfficientNet for multi-class classification) to classify skin cancers (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, melanoma) and benign tumors (seborrheic keratosis, melanocytic nevus) using clinical and dermoscopic images from the KCGMH and HAM10000 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and describes original experimental work, including the development of deep learning models (DenseNet and EfficientNet), retrospective review of patient records and clinical images from KCGMH and HAM10000 datasets, and reporting of new performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) for skin cancer classification.

title: Development of a light-weight deep learning model for cloud applications and remote diagnosis of skin cancers
brief_summary: This study developed a light-weight deep learning model to aid in the remote diagnosis of skin cancers and benign tumors using clinical and dermoscopic images. The researchers utilized DenseNet for binary classification and EfficientNet for multi-class classification, training and validating the models on the KCGMH and HAM10000 datasets. The DenseNet model achieved an accuracy of 89.5% for binary classification (benign vs. malignant) on the KCGMH dataset. For multi-class classification, EfficientNet-B4 achieved an accuracy of 85.8% on the seven-class HAM10000 dataset and 72.1% on the five-class KCGMH dataset. The results demonstrate that the proposed deep learning models are highly promising aids for the clinical diagnosis and early identification of skin cancers and benign tumors.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied in both KCGMH (n=430 pigmented, n=387 non-pigmented implied by total 430 in Table 1, though text says n=430; Table 1 shows 430) and HAM10000 (n=484) datasets. Used in binary (malignant) and multi-class classification tasks.
Studied in KCGMH dataset (n=185). Explicitly noted that KCGMH SCC does not include Bowen's disease or actinic keratosis, unlike HAM10000's AKIEC group. Used in five-class classification in KCGMH.
Studied in both KCGMH (n=78) and HAM10000 (n=1074) datasets. Used in binary (malignant) and multi-class classification tasks.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of benign vs. malignant skin tumors and multi-class classification of specific skin cancer types (BCC, SCC, MEL, BKL, NV, AKIEC, DF, VAS).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the aim to build a 'skin cancer classification model' using DenseNet for binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and EfficientNet for multi-class classification (seven-class in HAM10000 and five-class in KCGMH). The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (e.g., basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, seborrheic keratosis) rather than continuous values. Performance metrics reported include accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and AUC, which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large public collection of dermatoscopic images of skin cancer diagnostic categories, consisting of 10,015 images. It includes seven classes: actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen’s disease (AKIEC), basal cell carcinoma (BCC), benign keratosis-like lesions (BKL), dermatofibroma (DF), melanoma (MEL), melanocytic nevi (NV), and vascular lesions (VAS).

use.training: Used for training and validation of the EfficientNet-B4 multi-class classification model (seven-class). The dataset was split into training, validation, and testing sets at a ratio of 8:1:1.
use.validation: Used for validation of the EfficientNet-B4 multi-class classification model (seven-class).
use.testing: Used for testing the EfficientNet-B4 multi-class classification model (seven-class).

datasets_used.name: KCGMH
datasets_used.description: A private dataset from the Department of Dermatology at Kaohsiung Chang Gung Memorial Hospital (KCGMH), Taiwan. It consists of 1,287 clinical images of skin lesions from patients who received a histological diagnosis between 2006 and 2017. The images were taken by digital cameras and manually cropped. It includes five classes: basal cell carcinoma (BCC), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), melanoma (MEL), benign keratosis-like lesions (BKL), and melanocytic nevi (NV).

use.training: Used for training the DenseNet-121 binary classification model (benign vs. malignant) and the EfficientNet-B4 five-class classification model. The dataset was split into training, validation, and testing sets at a ratio of 8:1:1.
use.validation: Used for validation of the DenseNet-121 binary classification model and the EfficientNet-B4 five-class classification model.
use.testing: Used for testing the DenseNet-121 binary classification model and the EfficientNet-B4 five-class classification model.
use.other: Used to validate model performance in practical medical application using historical skin-lesion images taken by digital cameras.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver–operator curve (ROC) and Area under the receiver–operator curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the diagnostic performance of the deep learning models (DenseNet and EfficientNet) for binary and multi-class classification of skin cancers.
To find the optimal hyper-parameters for the EfficientNet model on the validation set.
To address data imbalance issues during model training, forcing the model to be more balanced across classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive/negative predictive values, and AUC.

KCGMH_binary_classification.description: Binary classification of benign vs. malignant skin tumors using DenseNet-121
KCGMH_binary_classification.notes: The model was trained with 200 epochs, a learning rate of 0.001, and a batch size of 30. The dataset was split into training, validation, and testing sets at a ratio of 8:1:1.

HAM10000_seven_class_classification.description: Seven-class classification of skin cancer types using EfficientNet-B4
HAM10000_seven_class_classification.notes: The model was trained for 100 epochs with a batch size of 64 and an initial learning rate of 0.001. Hyperparameters were optimized via grid search on the validation set. The dataset was split into training, validation, and testing sets at a ratio of 8:1:1.

KCGMH_five_class_classification.description: Five-class classification of skin lesions using EfficientNet-B4
KCGMH_five_class_classification.notes: The model was trained for 100 epochs with a batch size of 64 and an initial learning rate of 0.001. A weighted method was imposed to address data imbalance. The dataset was split into training, validation, and testing sets at a ratio of 8:1:1.

KCGMH_five_class_baseline_comparisons.description: Comparison of EfficientNet variants and other deep learning models on the KCGMH five-class classification task
KCGMH_five_class_baseline_comparisons.notes: EfficientNet-B4 was selected as the best backbone considering the balance between classification capability and feasibility for application deployment. The paper notes that DenseNet-121's five-class classification did not perform as well as its binary classification.

architecture_used.main_model: DenseNet-121 (for binary classification) and EfficientNet-B4 (for multi-class classification)
architecture_used.pretraining: All pretrained models in EfficientNet were trained on the ImageNet dataset (1000 classes). DenseNet details on pretraining are not explicitly stated, but it is a CNN-based method.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels and 112x112 pixels (images were resized to these dimensions)

components: Dense blocks (concatenate previous feature maps iteratively)
Transition layers (compress feature maps from dense blocks)
DenseNet.description: Representative CNN with less computations than ResNet, using shortcuts where output from each layer in dense blocks concatenates inputs of each subsequent layer.

EfficientNet.type: EfficientNet family (B1 to B7), with B4 selected as optimal
EfficientNet.scaling_method: Compound scaling of network depth, width, and resolution
EfficientNet.selection_criteria: Chosen based on balance between classification capability and feasibility for application deployment (light-weight)
EfficientNet.description: Light-weight neural network architecture. B0 is smallest, B7 is largest. B4 selected as moderate structure to prevent overfitting.

DenseNet-121.optimizer: Not explicitly named for DenseNet, but hyperparameters obtained by trials/empirical research

EfficientNet-B4.imbalance_handling: Weighted method imposed to address data imbalance

classification_or_output_head.DenseNet: Global average pooling, fully-connected (FC) layer, and softmax
classification_or_output_head.EfficientNet: Softmax (implied by classification task and comparison with other models using similar heads)

approximate_parameters.EfficientNet_B0: Smallest in family (parameter count not explicitly given, but B4 is 17.55M)
approximate_parameters.EfficientNet_B7: Largest in family (parameter count not explicitly given)

conclusions: The developed light-weight deep learning model (DenseNet for binary classification and EfficientNet-B4 for multi-class classification) is a highly promising aid for the clinical diagnosis and early identification of skin cancers and benign tumors.

The DenseNet-121 model achieved an accuracy of 89.5% for binary classification (benign vs. malignant) on the KCGMH dataset, demonstrating its effectiveness for identifying skin cancers and benign tumors.
The EfficientNet-B4 model achieved an overall accuracy of 85.8% for seven-class classification on the HAM10000 dataset and 72.1% for five-class classification on the KCGMH dataset.

EfficientNet-B4 was selected as the optimal architecture for multi-class classification because it provides the best balance between classification capability and feasibility for deployment on cloud platforms and mobile devices (light-weight architecture).
The study validates the feasibility of deploying deep learning models for remote diagnostic applications in skin cancer classification.
The model addresses data imbalance issues by imposing a weighted method, which helps prevent the model from neglecting minority classes such as melanoma.

The study highlights limitations in previous AI research, including a lack of testing for consistency, lack of pathological proof, and ambiguous comparisons, which this study aims to address by using histologically diagnosed datasets.
important_remarks: The study is limited by its retrospective design and reliance on a single-institution dataset (KCGMH) for clinical validation, which may introduce selection bias and limit generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

There is a significant class imbalance in the KCGMH dataset, particularly for melanoma (n=78) and squamous cell carcinoma (n=185), which the authors addressed using a weighted method but which may still affect model robustness for minority classes.

The KCGMH dataset includes clinical images taken by digital cameras, whereas the HAM10000 dataset consists of dermatoscopic images; this modality difference limits direct comparison and raises concerns about the model's performance on non-dermoscopic clinical images in real-world settings.

The definition of Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) in the KCGMH dataset does not include Bowen's disease or actinic keratosis, whereas the HAM10000 dataset groups these into the AKIEC category, creating a discrepancy in disease categorization between the two datasets.

The five-class classification performance on the KCGMH dataset was notably lower (72.1% accuracy) compared to the binary classification (89.5% accuracy) and the HAM10000 seven-class classification (85.8% accuracy), suggesting that the smaller, single-institution dataset may not provide sufficient data diversity for complex multi-class discrimination.

The study acknowledges that previous AI research often lacks testing for consistency, pathological proof, or ambiguous comparisons; while this study uses histologically diagnosed data, it does not report external validation on independent datasets beyond the provided splits.

The model was optimized for 'light-weight' deployment on cloud and mobile devices (EfficientNet-B4 with 17.55 million parameters), which may prioritize computational efficiency over maximum diagnostic accuracy compared to heavier models.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported to compare the performance of the proposed models against baseline or other deep learning architectures; comparisons were based on point estimates of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.
